data.title: Time to act on stalled proposal toughening child abuse penalties, lawmakers say
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have been urged to toughen penalties for child abuse as a proposal to review protection laws for minors, which were last updated in 1995, has remained stalled for seven years.
A check by the South China Morning Post found that over more than 20 years, only one of 13 serious child abuse cases involving ill-treatment or neglect of a child or young person resulted in a jail term close to the maximum of 10 years.
Lawmakers earlier this month called for increasing the 10-year maximum penalty for child abuse and neglect offences as they passed a non-binding motion on moving forward to “holistically review and enhance legislation relating to child protection”.
The maximum penalty for such offences was last increased from two years in 1995.
The SCMP looked at 13 judgments reached in the most serious child abuse and neglect cases between 1999 and last year, and found that only one adopted a starting point of nine years and six months’ imprisonment, while some resulted in sentences of about two years.
Three cases resulted in 10-year jail terms, but they also involved manslaughter charges.
Judges in several cases noted the court primarily needed to protect the vulnerable and deter would-be abusers.
However, one judge noted an appellate court had not set out any sentencing tariff, saying the circumstances of different cases varied.
A 2017 High Court case the SCMP examined involved a seven-year-old girl who was repeatedly assaulted by her stepmother, causing her to suffer irreversible brain damage and remain in a vegetative state.
At the trial, presiding judge Mr Justice Kevin Zervos said he noted that for some cases resulting in the death of a victim, a manslaughter charge could be laid against the offender.
But he said that for a case where the victim was still alive but had suffered permanent injury, the maximum sentence of 10 years’ imprisonment needed to be reviewed.
“This punishment does not fit the serious levels of this crime,” he said.
Zervos handed the stepmother nine years and six months in jail, while the father was sentenced to four years and six months for a separate offence of perverting the course of justice.
In 2019, the Law Reform Commission’s Causing or Allowing the Death of a Child or Vulnerable Adult Sub-committee published a consultation paper and a report two years later, recommending a stiffer sentence for child abuse offenders.
It suggested raising the maximum penalty to 20 years’ imprisonment in cases where the victim died and to 15 years if victims suffered permanent and serious harm.
At the Legislative Council meeting on April 1 and 2, lawmakers noted that the committee’s report has since been brushed aside, even as the number of child abuse and neglect reports logged by the Social Welfare Department rose from 940 in 2020 to 1,144 in 2025.
Legislator Nixie Lam Lam said Legco members agreed that a comprehensive review of the penalty for child abuse was needed.
“Most members agreed that the current sentence does not quite have a deterrent effect,” she said. “So should we begin from there?”
To push for a higher penalty, lawmakers would have to start a “long-term” discussion with authorities, she said.
She added that the Department of Justice would have to focus on reviewing all child-related provisions in existing legislation to identify gaps, including the dividing line for the age of children as it varied from 12 to 16 in different provisions.
Billy Wong Wai-yuk, executive secretary of the Hong Kong Committee on Children’s Rights, said the government’s “mindset” was to improve child protection laws “case by case” instead of speeding up legal reforms.
She noted the Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse Ordinance enacted in January encompassed more aspects of child protection and aimed to raise public awareness.

The new law requires 25 professions in the social welfare, education and medical sectors to report suspected maltreatment of minors or face penalties of up to three months in jail and a fine of HK$50,000 (US$6,410).
“Even with the implementation of the [new law], Hong Kong is still 20 to 30 years behind other jurisdictions that have a more comprehensive law on child protection,” she said.
Wong added that some child abuse offenders were only convicted of other offences, such as common assault, instead of ones that specifically aimed to protect children.
“It fails to send a clear message to the public that children are vulnerable people, causing a failure to bring justice,” she said.
Just last month, Kowloon City Court heard a sentencing review of a 49-year-old immigration officer who originally received 200 hours of community service for six counts of common assault. He had choked his wife and young son during domestic disputes between 2021 and 2025.
The presiding magistrate accepted the defence’s pleading and changed the penalty to one year of probation after hearing that performing community service would result in the officer losing his job and collaterally affecting the victims.
Lawmaker Lam questioned the sentence’s effect.
“Does the sentence have a deterrent effect on offenders with such behavioural issues?” she asked.
She added that a higher penalty could hopefully deter repeat offenders.

data.title: Hong Kong’s ministerial team spent HK$46.6 million on visits in past 3 years
data.description:
Hong Kong’s 21 ministerial-level officials spent more than HK$46.6 million (US$5.9 million) on trips over the past three financial years, with finance chief Paul Chan Mo-po accounting for nearly a quarter of the total.
Secretary for Commerce and Economic Development Algernon Yau Ying-wah was the most frequent traveller, making 50 trips in the past three years at a cost of HK$7.21 million to taxpayers, official records released to legislators showed.
Chan made 35 visits during the three financial years running from 2023 to 2026, travelling outside the city almost once a month on average. His trips incurred a total cost of HK$11.95 million – the highest among political appointees.
The destinations included Peru, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Georgia, Australia, Britain, Switzerland, the United States and mainland China.
He was typically accompanied by between one and five staff members on each trip, with the average cost exceeding HK$341,000.
Chan’s deputy, Michael Wong Wai-lun, travelled five times, mainly to the mainland and other parts of Asia, at a total cost of HK$470,000.
The official records were made available to legislators ahead of this week’s special finance committee meetings.

Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu recorded the second-highest travel expenditure, spending HK$5 million on 34 trips over the three-year period.
No 2 official Eric Chan Kwok-ki undertook 24 visits, costing HK$1.3 million. His deputy, Warner Cheuk Wing-hing, made five visits to the mainland and Macau at a cost of HK$60,000 – the lowest expenditure among all political appointees.
Former lawmaker Tik Chi-yuen, chairman of the centrist Third Side party, called for the Audit Commission to conduct a value-for-money audit.
“Residents have the right to know whether these trips are effective or just a waste of taxpayer money,” he said.
“On the one hand, the government cuts our welfare, saying we have a deficit. On the other hand, our officials keep flying here and there, rubbing shoulders with overseas business leaders and officials.
“We deserve to know what they have actually done over there and what economic or political benefits their visits have brought to Hong Kong.”
The Constitutional and Mainland Affairs Bureau, which prepared the documents, said the officials would “make the most use of their time during visits … to meet with relevant local officials and organisations”.
They would seek “to deepen mutual understanding, explore opportunities for cooperation and tell good stories of China and Hong Kong”, it added.
“Visits and study tours outside Hong Kong mostly focus on establishing connections, enhancing understanding, promoting Hong Kong’s advantages and cultivating interest in partnership.”
It added that it was not always possible to quantify the outcomes of each visit.
“Whether the governments and enterprises concerned will ultimately collaborate with the [Hong Kong] government and the business sector … depends on multiple factors, including the international landscape, policy and investment considerations, and the willingness and readiness of the parties involved,” it said.
“Such collaboration may not be forged directly through one single visit alone.”
Since becoming leader in 2022, John Lee Ka-chiu has been eager to rebuild the city’s image by telling “true” and “good stories of Hong Kong” to the international community.
His administration has sought to showcase the city’s position as a bridge connecting the mainland and the rest of the world, while seeking to attract investment after the Covid-19 economic slump and counter negative international perceptions of Hong Kong following the adoption of the national security law.

data.title: Alibaba’s Qwen family captures over 50% of global open-source downloads: reportQwen hits nearly 1 billion cumulative downloads, far surpassing rivals like Meta Platforms’ Llama and DeepSeek, researche
data.description:
Alibaba Cloud captured more than 50 per cent of global open-source model downloads as of March following the release of its Qwen 3.5 model series, a new report has found.
The finding underscores the dominance of Chinese models in the global open-source artificial intelligence landscape, though some US companies including OpenAI and Nvidia are also making early gains.
Qwen reached nearly 1 billion cumulative downloads by March, far surpassing rivals like Meta Platforms’ Llama and DeepSeek, according to researchers at Interconnects AI, a US-based newsletter tracking the global open-source AI landscape, in a report released on Wednesday.
Citing figures from developer platform Hugging Face, the researchers said Qwen alone generated 153.6 million downloads in February, more than double the combined total from the next eight major players, including Meta, DeepSeek and OpenAI.
Alibaba Cloud open-sourced Qwen 3.5, its latest flagship model series, in February, saying it was on par with leading US models from the likes of OpenAI and Anthropic. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.

Chinese models overtook their US counterparts last summer to become the most downloaded open-source models on Hugging Face. The shift away from popular US models such as Llama began in September 2024 with the release of Qwen 2.5, the report said.
Since then, several Chinese AI companies – including the Hong Kong-listed duo of MiniMax and Zhipu AI – have leaned into open-source as part of their global marketing push.
Even so, the share of global downloads achieved by other Chinese players had remained only a “fraction of the adoption level” of Qwen, with its lead further cemented by the release of Qwen 3.5, the researchers said.
Florian Brand, one of the report’s authors, said Qwen’s dominance was due to the “extreme popularity” of its smaller model versions of fewer than 10 billion parameters, which enabled developers globally to freely customise and deploy them at low cost.
Beijing has embraced open-source as a tool to spread AI broadly across its economy, in contrast to the closed-source approach favoured by US tech giants.
Several US players had nonetheless enabled a resurgence in open-source models in 2026, including Nvidia’s Nemotron and OpenAI’s GPT-OSS, both of which had seen “stickiness” in usage over time, according to Brand.
This contrasts with some Chinese models such as MiniMax’s M2.1, which saw spikes in downloads in the first few days but then fell quickly.
Still, the combined total of US model downloads stood at around 56 million as of March, compared with Qwen’s 942.1 million, the report said.
Open-source has emerged as a key battleground in the US-China AI competition, with both Beijing and Washington eager to make their tech stack the default infrastructure for the global AI boom.
A key question, however, is whether industry players will continue open-sourcing their models amid the rising computational demands of training cutting-edge models.
On Wednesday, Meta released Muse Spark, its first flagship model since it abandoned its open-source Llama series last April following a disappointing release. The new model was released as proprietary, though the company said it hoped to open-source “future versions”.
Meanwhile, Chinese companies including Alibaba Cloud and Zhipu have also kept some of their latest models closed-source as they seek to capture more usage directly through revenue-generating channels.

data.title: Medical intern suspended after complaint over patient data in social media post
data.description:
Hong Kong’s public hospital operator has suspended a medical intern and launched an investigation following a complaint that patient information had been exposed on social media, just days after another doctor was dismissed in a separate but similar privacy controversy that triggered citywide concern.
The Hospital Authority said on Saturday that the case involved an intern at Princess Margaret Hospital in Kwai Chung, who had since been temporarily taken off clinical duties.
“As a provider of public healthcare services, the Hospital Authority requires all staff to strictly adhere to professional codes, uphold integrity, and safeguard patient rights and privacy,” a spokesman said.
“We do not tolerate any form of disclosure of patient information.”
The authority said the hospital had received a public complaint on Friday and “immediately reviewed the incident and initiated an investigation”.
The intern, from the department of medicine and geriatrics, would be placed on leave starting on Sunday, the spokesman added.
The authority said it had also contacted the intern’s university and would handle the case in accordance with established procedures.
If a breach was confirmed, it would take disciplinary action and refer the case to the Medical Council if deemed necessary.
“Healthcare staff must maintain professionalism at all times when using social media, and place patients’ interests as the foremost priority,” the spokesman said, noting that further training would be strengthened in collaboration with medical schools.

According to a source, the complaint involved an intern suspected of posting a photo on a personal social media account, which showed a screen from the authority’s Clinical Management System (CMS), with patient information visible.
This case comes less than a week after another doctor was fired following a separate social media controversy that drew widespread criticism over patient privacy and the professional ethics of healthcare workers.
The authority’s Kowloon West group of hospitals announced on Tuesday that it had terminated the doctor’s contract, one day after stating he was “no longer an employee”.
The action followed media reports that Jenson So, a doctor and a former contestant on ViuTV’s reality show King Maker III, had been removed from internal hospital WhatsApp groups and was not included in next month’s duty roster.
So sparked social media outcry last month when he posted an Instagram story of himself performing a resuscitation in what appeared to be a public hospital ward.

data.title: Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs wraps up second bay area visit in 3 weeks
data.description:
Beijing’s top official overseeing Hong Kong affairs has concluded his second visit to the Greater Bay Area in three weeks, a trip that analysts view as a push from the central government to speed up the city’s integration with national development.
Xia Baolong, director of the State Council’s Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office, was in Guangdong province earlier this week, visiting Shenzhen, Dongguan and Huizhou.
State-run media said his four-day “research tour” focused on supporting Hong Kong and Macau “in proactively aligning with the national 15th five-year plan, deepening their participation in the development of the Greater Bay Area, and better integrating into and serving the country’s overall development strategy”.
Xia visited Guangzhou, Zhaoqing and Foshan on a similar mission in late March.
On both occasions, Xia was accompanied by his deputy Xu Qifang and Zhang Yong, deputy director of the central government’s liaison office in Hong Kong.
Pictures released by Xia’s office also showed he was joined in Shenzhen or Dongguan by Hong Kong health minister Lo Chung-mau, deputy finance minister Michael Wong Wai-lun, financial services secretary Christopher Hui Ching-yu and transport minister Mable Chan.
Xia’s latest trip included visits to a hospital, stock exchange and a logistics park.
Lau Siu-kai, a consultant at the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said Xia’s frequent visits to bay area cities showed Beijing attached deep importance to the region’s development.
“Beijing probably wants Hong Kong’s integration and participation with the region to be sped up,” Lau said. “That may need the push and guidance from the top level of the central government.”
The bay area is Beijing’s initiative to turn Hong Kong, Macau and nine Guangdong cities into an integrated economic hub.
China’s 15th five-year plan also positions the bay area as a critical engine for “new quality productive forces”, focusing on high-level innovation, industrial modernisation and deep regional integration.

Key goals include building a world-class bay area through smart manufacturing, digital development and enhanced cooperation in finance, talent and technology.
Hung Wing-lok, a political scientist at the Chinese University of Hong Kong’s school of governance and policy science, shared Lau’s views.
“The bay area is a large area and it is important [for Xia] to visit more to observe the progress of its development,” Hung said. “Beijing is possibly concerned about the slow progress of Hong Kong’s integration with bay area cities and Xia would like to know the challenges Hong Kong has faced.”
Veteran China watcher Hui Ching, research director of policy think tank the Hong Kong Zhi Ming Institute, said the central government’s leadership and coordination would be vital for the bay area development.
“The different social and political systems of Hong Kong, Macau and Guangdong make the integration or cooperation among themselves difficult,” he said.
“The bay area operates under a ‘one country, two systems’ and three jurisdictions structure. It involves the mainland’s civil law, Hong Kong’s common law and Macau’s Portuguese-influenced civil law.
“I am not suggesting that Beijing’s decision to develop the Greater Bay Area is wrong. The area has great geographic advantages. But the development will take a long time and possibly needs more coordination from the central government or Xia.”
Hung added Xia might also be concerned about the impact of the current Middle East crisis on the bay area’s development.
“This includes whether some ongoing projects could be affected by the war and perhaps the potential benefits the bay area could derive,” he said.

data.title: Gucci shoes worth HK$11,000 stolen from shopping centre restroom in Hong Kong
data.description:
Hong Kong police are investigating after a pair of Gucci shoes worth HK$11,000 (US$1,404) were stolen from a restroom at Times Square in Causeway Bay, the South China Morning Post has learned.
A source said on Saturday that the victim, 48, had bought the shoes from the Gucci store on the second floor of the shopping centre before heading to the restroom on the third floor.
She left the shoes behind, and when she returned, she found the shoebox, but the footwear was missing, the source said.
Police were alerted at 12.43pm by a security guard about a woman losing a pair of shoes at a shopping centre at No 1 Matheson Street.
After a preliminary investigation, police suspected the shoes worth about HK$11,000 were stolen from the woman in a restroom of the shopping centre, according to the force.
Police said the case had been classified as theft and was being followed up by the force’s Wan Chai division, adding that no arrest had been made.
In Hong Kong, theft is punishable by up to 10 years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Turtle found dead after apparent fall in Hong Kong’s Wong Tai Sin
data.description:
A 10cm-long (4-inch) turtle has died after apparently falling in Hong Kong’s Wong Tai Sin.
Police said on Saturday that they were alerted at 2.09pm by a security guard about a turtle suspected of having fallen from a height at No 185 Hammer Hill Road.
Online footage and images showed the turtle lying motionless on a playground mat.
In Hong Kong, animal cruelty is punishable by up to three years in jail and a fine of HK$200,000 (US$25,530) upon conviction.

data.title: Landlords of 5,557 subdivided homes seek 3-year grace period to fix flats
data.description:
Landlords of more than 5,500 subdivided homes in Hong Kong have applied for a three-year grace period to meet size, safety and hygiene requirements following a new law on basic housing standards taking effect six weeks ago.
Secretary for Housing Winnie Ho Wing-yin said on Saturday that the government had received applications for 5,557 subdivided units in 1,370 flats. That accounted for around 5 per cent of all subdivided units in Hong Kong, mostly located in Yau Tsim Mong district, followed by Sham Shui Po and Eastern.
Ho said 780 subdivided units had been granted a grace period, with their details uploaded onto the Housing Bureau’s website.
“When landlords divided their flats, they not only spent money to add washrooms and pipes but also incurred safety, health and fire risks. If something happens to your elderly tenant, your property value will be affected too,” she said.
“We hope to set standards and allow landlords to review if their subdivided flats meet the standards.”




Asked if the rules would affect the supply of homes on the market and push up rents, Ho disagreed.
“The current administration has substantially increased home supply and shortened the wait for public rental housing,” she said.
“With 50,000 flats from transitional housing and light public housing as a buffer, the tenants know what the right choices are … they will be paying HK$5,000 less for a better environment.”
Ho added that the government had been monitoring subdivided flat rents, and they had been largely stable despite some fluctuations.
The Basic Housing Units Ordinance took effect on March 1. Under the new legislation, only “basic housing units” – certified subdivided homes that meet minimum standards – will be allowed on the market for leasing.

Homes are required to have a minimum internal floor area of eight square metres, a minimum height of 2.3 metres from floor to ceiling, an enclosed, waterproof toilet and at least one openable window, among others.
However, landlords have one year to register for the grace period. They have three years under the grace period to rectify their substandard properties while continuing to lease them.
Those who file the application by August 31 this year will have their application fee waived.
Ho urged landlords to file their applications as soon as possible, saying she expected an influx right before the early-bird discount ended and before the final deadline.
If subdivided flat tenants faced difficulties in finding a new home, they could contact district service teams to seek help, Ho said.
“We know many subdivided flat tenants, especially the older ones, lack access to information,” Ho said.
“We’ve seen cases who are eligible for public rental housing but had no idea, so our district service teams helped with the applications and referred them to transitional housing or light public housing.”
On light public housing, Ho said the government had received almost 33,000 applications for the 30,000 flats, with priority given to those with special needs, such as those with children.


Light public housing is temporary, low-cost modular homes for families waiting at least three years for a traditional public rental flat.
She said all flats were occupied and the light public housing turnover rate had reached 104 per cent as more than 200 households had moved to traditional public housing. Some bought subsidised homes.
“We believe that the turnover will continue to increase. For transitional housing, the turnover has reached 140 per cent, which means 40 per cent of flats have been left to other families in need.”
Ho said the waiting time for public rental housing – currently 5.1 years – would continue to fall towards the target of 4.5 years with the government’s multipronged effort to increase supply.
She said the government allocated 20,600 flats last year, while 14,000 joined the queue.
“We are seeing a turnaround in the situation where the number of allocations exceeds new entries by more than 6,000. We are truly clearing the queue … The waiting list shows a distinct downward trend. This is a very positive sign.”
She said she expected the queue to shorten further in the second half of the year as a new light public housing project in Chai Wan would be completed.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Cathay Pacific and HK Express cut some flights as jet fuel prices rise
data.description:
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways will cancel a small proportion of passenger flights between mid-May and the end of June, as surging jet fuel prices driven by the war in the Middle East continue to weigh on airlines globally.
The airline said on Saturday that about 2 per cent of its total flight frequencies would be cut between May 16 and June 30, mainly on regional routes, as well as a small number of services to Australia, South Asia and South Africa.
Cathay Pacific’s budget arm, HK Express, will also cancel about 6 per cent of flights from May 11 to June 30.
The company said cutting back capacity had always been its “last resort”, but it had to consolidate the passenger flights “to mitigate part of the increased costs”.
“All affected customers will be offered protection onto flights departing within 24 hours of their originally scheduled flights,” an airline spokesman said, adding they would be notified of new arrangements by April 13.
“The ongoing volatile situation in the Middle East continues to negatively impact the price of jet fuel … This is placing huge cost pressure on airlines around the world.”
The airline also extended the suspension of its Dubai and Riyadh routes until June 30, as conflict in the Middle East continued to disrupt operations and push up the cost of fuel.
On March 26, the airline announced it would increase its fuel surcharge for all flights by 34 per cent from April – the second increase in two weeks – citing “considerable pressure” as the war in the Middle East affected oil prices.
On April 1, the surcharge for short-haul flights rose from HK$290 (US$37) to HK$389, and for medium-haul flights from HK$541 to HK$725.
The fuel surcharge for long-haul flights increased by HK$396 to HK$1,560, or by 34 per cent from HK$1,164.

data.title: Hins Cheung to lead arrested 2019 Hong Kong protesters on mainland exchange trips
data.description:
Cantopop singer Hins Cheung, who previously expressed support for social movements in Hong Kong, will lead young people arrested during the 2019 anti-government protests on visits to mainland China to witness the country’s prosperity and serve as a mentor for a “special rehabilitation project” under the Security Bureau.
Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung also revealed on Saturday that the project, which had been running over the past two years, targeted about 7,000 people arrested but not prosecuted during the protests.
In an interview with Wen Wei Po published on Saturday, the 45-year-old singer expressed “deep repentance” for his past inappropriate words and actions, saying he would personally lead youth exchange visits to the mainland during the first half of the year.
He also admitted that he was “impetuous in his youth”, lacked a “thorough understanding of the social situation”, and added that some of his past performances were “inappropriate” and led the public to doubt his patriotism.
Cheung had voiced support for the protests against national education in 2012, and the Occupy Central movement in 2014, where he sang the movement’s theme song with actor Anthony Wong Chau-sang.
“I know many people have questioned my love for my country and Hong Kong because of this. Today, I offer my sincere apology,” he said.
“I was hot-headed and impulsive when I was younger … I deeply regret my past ignorant words and actions.”
Cheung said that as a public figure, his “most basic social responsibility” was to convey a clear and firm stance to the public. He added that he hoped to use his status to encourage young people and “proactively expressed his willingness” to participate in the bureau’s programme.
He said he believed that society would give truly remorseful young people a chance.
By sharing his personal journey with the arrested youths, Cheung hoped to set a positive example and show that admitting past shortcomings and correcting them was important.
In response to queries by the South China Morning Post on the singer’s involvement in the programme, the Security Bureau said police had proactively contacted youths involved in the 2019 protests under legally compliant circumstances to guide them through positive activities, encouraging critical thinking and the ability to distinguish right from wrong.
“These efforts provide them with an opportunity to turn over a new leaf and start afresh. However, the specific details of these operations cannot be disclosed,” the bureau said in a statement.
Born in 1981 in Guangzhou, in the mainland’s Guangdong province, Cheung signed with a Hong Kong music label in 2002 and made a name for himself in the city’s Cantopop scene.
Cheung was previously embroiled in a patriotism controversy, after being accused of backing Hong Kong and Taiwanese independence movements.

In 2017, he was labelled a secessionist “mouse dropping” by China National Defence Daily – a paper run by the Ministry of National Defence.
He was banned from performing on the mainland television show I Am A Singer, and had denied accusations he supported Hong Kong independence.
In a televised interview on Saturday, security chief Tang said the project aimed to rehabilitate arrested protesters through “non-prosecutorial, legally compliant means.”
“The activities include helping them gain a better understanding of the country and national security,” Tang said.
“Some were very fearful, not knowing if they could travel to mainland China or if they would face trouble there. We will assist them and even bring them there.”
He said the government also offered these people internship opportunities within departments.
“If they meet the requirements, we believe it is not necessarily impossible for them to become civil servants,” he said.
When asked if this meant they definitely would not face prosecution, Tang said the rehabilitation programme handled these protesters in a way that “did not require going to court,” provided it complied with the law.
Regarding how authorities determined if an individual was truly remorseful, he said participants had to write reflections or share their experiences, discussing how they were incited during the protests.
He added that a mechanism was in place to decide who would receive these opportunities, though he declined to disclose details.

data.title: Calls grow for HKMA to go further on stablecoins after conservative first batch
data.description:
The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) is being urged to go further in loosening restrictions on stablecoins, after granting the city’s first two licences to banks to issue the digital currency.
Bankers, venture capitalists and analysts said the long-awaited approvals – awarded to HSBC and a joint venture led by Standard Chartered – fell short of market expectations, underscoring the regulator’s cautious stance amid lingering risk concerns.
“It came as a surprise that only two licences were issued, and both to traditional banks,” said Kenny Ng Lai-yin, a strategist at Everbright Securities International. “The market had expected the authority to issue at least three licences for stablecoin issuers from a broader range of backgrounds.”
Ng said the HKMA was prioritising risk management, even as it sought to promote financial innovation through stablecoins.
Unlike highly volatile cryptocurrencies such as bitcoin and Ethereum, stablecoins are typically pegged to fiat currencies or other reference assets, with the aim of combining the efficiency of digital assets with the stability of traditional money.

Hong Kong is among the first jurisdictions to introduce a comprehensive regulatory framework for stablecoin issuance. Its Stablecoin Ordinance – one of the world’s earliest such laws – came into effect in August last year, reinforcing the city’s ambitions to become a global digital asset hub.
“We believe this signals a conservative regulatory approach, with banks – rather than less-regulated players – at the forefront of market development,” S&P Global Ratings said in a research note following the announcement.
The approvals also arrived later than expected. The HKMA had previously indicated it would issue only a small number of licences after reviewing 36 applications.
Deputy CEO Darryl Chan Wai-man said the delay reflected a longer-than-anticipated review process, as regulators sought to ensure applicants met stringent requirements on risk management, know-your-customer checks and operational resilience.
Industry participants are now calling for broader access in future rounds.
David Chang, founder and CEO of venture capital firm MindWorks, said licences for non-bank issuers and technology start-ups would be a welcome next step alongside further deregulation.
“At the moment, only banks have been licensed, and only Hong Kong dollar-pegged stablecoins are permitted,” he said. “We hope the next batch will include more non-bank companies and start-ups.”
The HKMA selected HSBC and the Standard Chartered-led consortium in part because both were among the city’s note-issuing banks, with established experience in currency issuance and maintaining financial stability, said Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators.
“We respect the results of the HKMA’s first batch of licensed stablecoin issuers,” RD Technologies, a fintech firm founded by former HKMA CEO Norman Chan Tak-lam, said in a statement.
“We will continue to maintain close communication with regulatory bodies and look forward to achieving progress in the next phase of regulatory assessment and license issuance.”

RD Technologies was among participants in the HKMA’s stablecoin issuer sandbox, launched in 2024 to allow limited testing across use cases including e-commerce payments, cross-border settlements and tokenised asset trading.
HSBC said on Friday it planned to launch a Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin in the second half of this year, integrating it into its PayMe and mobile banking platforms. Initial use cases will include peer-to-peer transfers and merchant payments.
Meanwhile, Anchorpoint – the joint venture formed by Standard Chartered, Animoca Brands and Hong Kong Telecommunications – aimed to roll out its Hong Kong dollar stablecoin, HKD At Par (HKDAP), in the second quarter, initially targeting institutional investors, CEO Dominic Maffei said in a media briefing on Friday.
Stablecoin-related products could expand banks’ fee income and product offerings, according to S&P. They may also help lenders attract mainland Chinese clients seeking offshore crypto exposure, given restrictions on such assets in the domestic market.

data.title: China healthcare stocks shine in Hong Kong amid Middle East turmoil
data.description:
China’s healthcare sector has been drawing offshore capital to Hong Kong-listed stocks as investors look for safe havens amid global volatility in commodities.
The Hang Seng Healthcare Index, tracking some of China’s most innovative pharmaceutical companies including Akeso and Innovent Biologics, has surged about 13 per cent since March 23, outpacing the benchmark Hang Seng Index’s about 6 per cent gain over the same period.
“Although the Middle East conflict has created a lot of uncertainty and volatility in the overall market, we have seen investor interest in China healthcare start to improve in the past two weeks, given that healthcare is generally more insulated from commodity prices than many other industries,” said Yang Huang, head of China healthcare research at JPMorgan Chase.
The healthcare stock rally comes as commodity markets have whipsawed since late February when the US and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, resulting in the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway through which one-fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas passes.
Brent crude jumped to a peak of US$121.88 per barrel in March before retreating to US$98.66 on Thursday, while West Texas Intermediate traded at US$97.40 a barrel.
The healthcare sector’s improving fundamentals were also providing a tailwind, according to analysts.
China’s biotech subsector posted revenue and net profit growth of 36 per cent and 103 per cent, respectively, in 2025, driven by commercialisation and licensing deals, Huang said.
“The healthcare sector shows resilient performance versus the broader Hong Kong market, contributed by stronger-than-expected earnings from large-cap pharmaceutical firms,” said Jonah Chen, head of healthcare research at China Merchants Securities (Hong Kong).

Innovative cancer drug makers Hansoh Pharmaceutical and Innovent were leading the sector’s outperformance after strong earnings releases, Chen added.
Among constituents of the Hang Seng Healthcare Index, Hansoh has surged 26 per cent since March 23 to HK$38.70 on Friday, while Akeso has jumped about 23 per cent to HK$138. Innovent advanced about 14 per cent to HK$88.20 over the span.
A slew of Chinese biopharmaceutical and biotech companies posted their first-ever annual profits in 2025 after years of losses caused by heavy investments in research and development.
Hong Kong-listed Innovent posted a net profit of 814 million yuan (US$119 million) in 2025, reversing a net loss of 94.6 million yuan in 2024, driven by the sales of 18 approved products, including the cancer therapy sintilimab and weight-loss drug mazdutide.
Shenzhen-based XtalPi Holdings recorded a net profit of 134.6 million yuan last year, swinging from a net loss of 1.51 billion yuan in 2024, driven by a 419 per cent surge in revenue from its artificial intelligence-powered drug discovery business.
Another notable case is Yantai-based Rongchang Biopharmaceutical, a developer of antibody-drug conjugates and biologics for cancer and autoimmune diseases, which swung from a net loss of 1.47 billion yuan in 2024 to a net profit of 710 million yuan in 2025.
Rongchang licensed its autoimmune drug telitacicept to US-listed Vor Biopharma and an eye drug to Japan’s Santen Pharmaceutical, contributing to a record US$135.7 billion in out-licensing deals by Chinese drug makers last year.

data.title: Hong Kong proposes mandatory renovation briefings to combat bid-rigging
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have proposed a legal amendment requiring property owners to attend a mandatory briefing on major renovation projects before voting on procurement resolutions, in a move aimed at curbing bid-rigging following the deadly blaze in Tai Po.
Speaking on a radio programme on Saturday, Secretary for Home and Youth Affairs Alice Mak Mei-kuen said the proposed amendment to the Building Management Ordinance, which covers five areas, sought to enhance homeowners’ participation and fill existing legal gaps.
“From our experience of handling disputes in recent years, we found that many conflicts arose from an information asymmetry,” she said.
“It would be helpful if homeowners did not rely solely on the general meeting to ask questions and make decisions, which would be too rushed … the briefing could provide more information for owners to make an informed decision and allow the management company or the management committee to address their questions before the general meeting.
“If they failed to do so, owners would be more motivated to attend the meeting on their own to vote.”
The fire, which broke out at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court on November 26, killed 168 people and displaced nearly 5,000 residents.
The tragedy at the estate, under renovation since July 2024, exposed flaws in building management and maintenance oversight, raising concerns over bid-rigging in the sector.
In response, the government proposed an amendment to the Building Management Ordinance.
It includes capping the number of proxies a person can hold. An individual would be allowed to represent only one owner in buildings with up to 50 flats, while in larger complexes the limit would be 2 per cent of the total owners, or 20, whichever was lower.
The change would require disclosure of which owners had given their endorsements 48 hours before a general meeting to prevent proxy forgery.

The amendment also calls for a tiered system for quorum and in-person voting thresholds, to be increased in line with project costs.
Contractors and consultants would be required to declare their interests under the plan.
Owners’ meetings would be subject to new rules, including a ban on committee chairmen changing the agenda or postponing meetings once they had started.
The government would also be granted powers to intervene if a management committee became unable to function.
Mak said a public consultation would be held on the proposed changes. Hong Kong last amended the ordinance in mid-2025, months before the fire.
She added that building management work groups established across the city’s 18 districts would assist in collecting views and conducting focus group interviews.
Mak said Hop On Management Company, the government-appointed administrator for Wang Fuk Court, was currently verifying each owner’s contribution to the maintenance fund in preparation for refunds.
Hop On will hold a briefing session in early May for owners and residents to discuss the refund.
The company was also reviewing existing contracts, including those involving fire safety facilities, to determine the legal and financial implications of early termination, she said.
As residents prepare to return to collect their belongings from April 20, Mak reminded them to be mindful of their physical limits, noting that climbing the stairs would be physically demanding.
She said some residents planned to borrow exoskeletons and warned that using such devices still required physical strength and skill.

data.title: What does China’s tightening grip on red-chip structures mean for IPOs?
data.description:
China’s red-chip structure – long used by internet companies to attract foreign capital while navigating domestic restrictions – is facing renewed scrutiny as regulators tighten oversight of offshore listings.
The shift is already reshaping the pipeline for Hong Kong initial public offerings (IPOs), with companies increasingly being encouraged to unwind these structures or justify why they remain necessary.
This explainer outlines the reasons behind the policy shift, what it means for tech IPOs, and what it takes to unwind the structure.
What is a red-chip structure?
The term “red chip” dates back to the 1980s, when Chinese state-backed firms used offshore entities to control Hong Kong-listed companies.
In the internet era, this evolved into the widely used variable interest entity (VIE) structure. It allows foreign investors to gain exposure to sectors such as telecoms and the internet, which are otherwise subject to restrictions on overseas ownership.
Typically, a company sets up an offshore holding entity – often in the Cayman Islands – which controls a Hong Kong subsidiary. That subsidiary, in turn, establishes a wholly foreign-owned enterprise (WFOE) in mainland China. Rather than owning the operating business outright, the WFOE controls it through contractual agreements.
The model was pioneered by Sina for its Nasdaq listing in 2000 and later adopted by major firms including Alibaba Group Holding and Tencent Holdings. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Despite gradual easing of foreign investment limits in certain pilot zones since 2014, the VIE structure has remained popular, largely because it allows profits from mainland operations to flow to offshore entities, benefiting international investors.
Why is Beijing tightening oversight?
Regulators have recently signalled a tougher stance, particularly for companies seeking to list in Hong Kong.
Some red-chip firms have been advised to restructure and pursue listings through their mainland entities instead. Where they retain offshore structures, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) is requiring companies to justify their necessity.
There is no formal rule change, but approval data points to a clear shift: only one red-chip company using a VIE structure – spatial design software developer Manycore Tech – received listing approval between January 1 and March 18 this year, compared with 21 in the same period last year.
One concern is that offshore structures could weaken regulatory visibility. Funds generated from overseas listings – including dividends and share sale proceeds – were often held outside China and could be difficult for authorities to track, according to legal analysts.
“Although Chinese policies impose certain requirements, it is common for these overseas funds to remain undisclosed and untraceable,” said Allbright Law Offices lawyers Fang Xiaojie and Bu Ping in an article last month.

This is not the first time Beijing has tightened oversight of the legally ambiguous offshore structure.
In 2020, regulators made clear that such entities were not exempt from antitrust rules governing internet platforms, and the State Administration of Market Regulation fined units of Alibaba Group Holding and Tencent Holdings, among others, for failing to seek approval for past deals involving offshore VIEs.
What does it take to unwind a red-chip structure?
Dismantling a red-chip structure is complex and time-consuming.
Companies must first sever ties between their offshore holding entities and their mainland operating businesses, typically requiring foreign investors to exit or convert their holdings into stakes in the domestic entity, according to an article by Beijing Dacheng Law Offices lawyers Joanna Liu and Xiao Yao.
This may involve share buy-backs or restructuring ownership arrangements. Offshore entities then need to be deregistered or sold.
The process can take one to two years, although some steps can run in parallel with IPO preparations, the lawyers said.
Costs can be significant. Companies may face tax liabilities and administrative expenses linked to liquidation, while also needing to manage large cash movements and minimise capital lock-up during the transition, they said.
What is the impact on IPOs?
The tightening stance was already slowing some Hong Kong listing plans, according to sources.
Companies with VIE structures have faced delays, while those that have restructured are being asked to provide detailed documentation on compliance – including foreign exchange, tax and overseas investment approvals.
The shift could also dampen foreign investor appetite. Moving from offshore structures to domestic entities introduces currency risks and complicates exit strategies for existing investors.
In turn, this may raise financing costs for Chinese companies and add another layer of uncertainty to the IPO pipeline.

data.title: Tighter civil service disciplinary rules risk becoming excessive: union head
data.description:
The head of a civil service union federation in Hong Kong has warned that tighter disciplinary action risks becoming excessive as the government seeks to revise the penalty system to handle underperforming workers this year.
Leung Chau-ting, chairman of the Federation of Civil Service Unions, was responding to the Civil Service Bureau’s plan to revise the regulations to further improve the disciplinary mechanism, including tightening rules on withholding salaries during suspensions and confiscating withheld pay.
Responding to a lawmaker’s inquiry, the bureau said it aimed to implement the revised Public Service (Administration) Order and the Public Service (Disciplinary) Regulation this year.
It also revealed that 151 civil servants were dismissed for serious misconduct or criminal offences between 2022 and 2025, with the number of dismissals dropping steadily from 60 in 2022-23 to 51 in 2023-24 and 40 in 2024-25.
Of the total, 73 were dismissed for criminal offences and 78 for misconduct. Dismissals among police officers were the highest at 44.
Leung told the South China Morning Post that tighter disciplinary action risked becoming excessive, with some supervisors acting aggressively under pressure to meet headcount reduction targets.
He said minor or non-core performance issues could increasingly be escalated into disciplinary cases, creating a perception that authorities were “tightening the net”.
While Leung described the number of dismissals in the last few years as “not particularly high”, he said the impact on the workforce might be broader.
“What concerns us more is the increasing number of those who join and then leave after a short time,” he said, adding the government was already faced with manpower pressures.
He cautioned that increasing pressure on frontline staff could further strain morale, particularly among younger recruits, potentially affecting the long-term stability of the civil service.
Lee Fong-chung, chairman of the Federation of HKSAR Civil Servants, said he supported authorities’ further efforts to improve the civil service management system and enhance overall effectiveness.
He said the 151 dismissals over the past three years represented only a small number of all government workers, adding clear regulations and rigorous procedures for handling disciplinary cases were in place.
“We welcome and support the Civil Service Bureau in enhancing the disciplinary mechanism and expediting the handling of relevant cases this year,” Lee said.
“It is expected that the authorities will communicate closely with civil service bodies and collect their views to jointly build a personnel management system that takes into account flexibility and efficiency.”
Lee added he believed that the government would continue to strike a balance between governance efficiency and the legitimate rights and interests of civil servants.
Under the existing disciplinary mechanism, if a civil servant is under investigation by his or her department for serious misconduct or by law enforcement for criminal offences, or if authorities started or intended to initiate disciplinary or criminal proceedings, the person could be suspended based on considerations of public interest.

The bureau added that suspension was an administrative measure instead of a disciplinary action.
If a suspended person was charged with misconduct or prosecuted for a criminal offence that could lead to dismissal, his or her salary could be withheld by up to half during the proceedings under the current mechanism.
The bureau said that if the person was convicted of a criminal offence that could lead to dismissal, his or her salary must be withheld in full by the government. If the employee was subsequently dismissed, the withheld salary during the suspension period would be forfeited with no recovery process.
In an effort to hold senior civil servants accountable for underperformance and build a more effective governing team, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu announced in last year’s policy address a new “Heads of Department Accountability System” to penalise underperforming senior civil servants and address internal deficiencies.
Under the new system, investigations will be divided into two tiers based on the severity of the issues. The first tier covers problems of a general nature, while the second addresses serious, widespread, repetitive or systemic issues.
Authorities will consider disclosing the findings of investigations in cases of public concern.

data.title: Discovery Bay developer submits proposal for full taxi access despite backlash
data.description:
The developer of Hong Kong’s Discovery Bay has proposed allowing unrestricted taxi access and adding four new ranks in the resort-style seaside community on Lantau Island, submitting a paper to the District Council despite recent resident backlash.
In the paper submitted to the Islands District Council, Hong Kong Resort Company said the current lack of point-to-point public transport “severely impacts residents’ daily lives” and that it had received hundreds of messages of support for the unrestricted taxi access proposal.
The company said existing restrictions limiting taxi access to Discovery Bay’s North Plaza created “significant transport barriers”, particularly for the elderly, those in poor health, people with mobility difficulties, carers with children, travellers with bulky luggage and late-night commuters.
Under its proposal, the company said it planned to allow urban and Lantau taxis to enter other areas of Discovery Bay and build new ranks at Discovery Bay Fire Station, Nim Shue Wan Kaito Pier, Discovery Bay Golf Club and Discovery Bay Multi-Recreation Centre.
A traffic monitoring system powered by artificial intelligence (AI) would be used to detect taxi drivers’ behaviour such as speeding, illegal overtaking and illegal parking, it added.
Hong Kong Resort Company also proposed “optimised pedestrian crossing facilities” near the four new taxi ranks to ensure the safety of schoolchildren and pedestrians.
“Besides implementing 24-hour duty shifts, security staff will patrol the main roads at least six times daily and conduct speed checks four times a day,” it said, adding that cabbies would receive verbal and written warnings for traffic violations and could be banned from entering Discovery Bay after repeated offences.
It also cited traffic incident data, saying about six minor incidents involving taxis occurred within Discovery Bay annually, with no serious injuries or fatalities reported.

The company said representatives from Discovery Bay, neighbouring Nim Shue Wan and the outlying island of Peng Chau – which has a connecting kaito service – collected around 4,000 residents’ signatures in January 2025, urging the government to improve taxi services.
It also cited a public consultation conducted from February 24 to March 16 that received around 6,000 signatures from residents in the three locations “expressing their full support” for point-to-point taxi service in Discovery Bay.
“HK Resort also received around 700 views, and 87 per cent of them supported and the remaining 12 per cent and 1 per cent opposed and were neutral respectively,” the company said, adding that it would later “explain the consultation results and respond to residents’ views”.
The proposal has recently faced pushback from Discovery Bay residents who say they prefer to maintain a car-free environment.
Residents told the South China Morning Post that the latest attempt to expand taxi access had been pushed through without adequate consultation.
Dana Winograd, a resident of 28 years, lodged a complaint earlier this month with the city’s ombudsman, expressing concern that the proposal had not been “handled appropriately” by the Transport Department.
A petition bearing 1,700 signatures has also been submitted to the department calling for proper public consultation and wider communication on the measures.
The council’s Traffic and Transport Committee will meet on Tuesday afternoon to discuss the paper.
Taxis have had limited access to Discovery Bay since 2014, restricted to the North Plaza of the residential area.
While there have been previous attempts to allow more taxis into the area, such proposals have met similar resistance from residents who say they prefer a car-free environment removed from Hong Kong’s typical urban sprawl.

data.title: Jardine Matheson to exit Taikoo Place, consolidate offices in Central: sources
data.description:
Jardine Matheson is giving up its remaining office space in FWD Tower at Taikoo Place and plans to relocate staff back to Jardine House in Central as part of operational consolidation, sources said.
The move reflects a wider trend of companies consolidating into the core district, where demand for premium office space has been strengthening.
The group’s primary office at Jardine House, part of its own property portfolio, “is now undergoing renovations to create a modern workspace that will accommodate all head office employees from May”, allowing it to operate “more collaboratively and dynamically”, according to sources familiar with the matter.
Jardine Matheson did not make an official comment on the relocation.
The conglomerate currently occupies one floor in FWD Tower. It had already given up one floor last year and the relocation would see it surrender the remaining space, sources said. The move was limited to the parent firm’s operations, they added.
DFI Retail Group, a unit under Jardine Matheson that operates Wellcome, Mannings, 7-Eleven and Ikea in Hong Kong, is listed on the fifth floor of FWD Tower in its 2025 annual report and is not affected.

Swire Properties, the landlord of Taikoo Place, confirmed Jardine Matheson’s current tenancy but declined to comment further.
Jardine Matheson has been reshaping parts of its business in recent years, reducing headcount and office arrangements across locations. The group controls a wide range of businesses, including holding major interests such as Hongkong Land, Mandarin Oriental, and Jardine Pacific.
The relocation aligns with a broader trend of companies consolidating into Central, where landlords such as Henderson Land have been positioning premium office space to capture renewed demand.
This shift is taking place against the backdrop of a diverging office market, with Central showing signs of recovery while decentralised districts continue to face pressure. Vacancy rates in Central fell to 9.9 per cent in February, returning to single digits for the first time in more than two years, while rents rose 3.5 per cent in the first two months, according to JLL.
By contrast, Hong Kong East – where Taikoo Place is located – has a vacancy rate of 12.8 per cent, reflecting softer demand as rent continues to face pressure.
“Taikoo Place continues to maintain a steady occupancy rate,” a Swire Properties spokesperson said, adding that its offices were well-positioned to benefit from the ongoing “flight to quality” trend.
Its Hong Kong office rental income fell 4 per cent year on year to HK$4.89 billion (US$624 million), with CEO Tim Blackburn warning that office demand remained subdued in the near term, according to its 2025 annual report.

data.title: Samsung weighs China overhaul as local rivals pile on the pressure
data.description:
Samsung Electronics faces a “strategic dilemma” over whether to defend its shrinking footprint in China or redeploy resources to bolster global competitiveness, analysts say, as speculation grows that the tech giant may scale back parts of its mainland operations and double down on semiconductors.
The South Korean firm was considering a broad restructuring of its China business, potentially exiting segments such as home appliances and displays while retaining smartphones and storage as core units, according to Chinese outlet Yicai, citing industry sources.
Samsung was likely to prioritise its semiconductor operations in China, even as it pares back other business lines, according to the report, which cited anonymous industry insiders.
The company has already been weighing a partial retreat, with job cuts under way in some underperforming divisions, according to sources who spoke to the South China Morning Post.
One source said Samsung could offload the distribution of home appliance sales to local partners, while retaining manufacturing operations. However, the decisions were not final and the scale of any exits remained unclear, another source said.
Samsung did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
“Samsung faces a strategic dilemma: put resources into defending market share in China or better competing against Chinese firms globally,” said Troy Stangarone, a non-resident fellow at the Carnegie Mellon Institute for Strategy and Technology.
“With intensifying competition and declining share in China’s domestic market, Samsung appears to have concluded its best strategy is to focus on global competition rather than competing head-on in China,” he said.

The reported shift contrasts with Samsung’s strong earnings momentum, driven by surging global demand for artificial intelligence chips and infrastructure.
The company this week forecast an eightfold jump in first-quarter operating profit to 57.2 trillion won (US$37.8 billion), a record level that would exceed its full-year earnings for 2025.
Samsung maintains a broad presence in China spanning semiconductors, consumer electronics and home appliances, alongside research and development centres in Beijing and Nanjing focused on artificial intelligence, telecommunications and smartphones.
At the same time, it has continued to invest heavily in its chip operations in the country. The company poured 465.4 billion won in its Xian chip plant in 2025, up 67.5 per cent from a year earlier, according to a filing with South Korea’s Financial Supervisory Service.
While Samsung’s home appliance business has been “gradually losing ground in China”, the restructuring was not intended as a pivot towards semiconductors, said Lee Byung-chul, a visiting research fellow at the Sejong Institute and former Samsung executive vice-president who worked at its China subsidiary for 15 years.
“It would be more accurate to say its position is shrinking further in response to the competitive environment in China,” Lee said.
Samsung is increasingly up against domestic rivals that are narrowing the technology gap while benefiting from state support, lower labour costs and looser regulatory constraints, Stangarone said.
“This reflects a broader strategic challenge for Korean firms,” he added.
Additional reporting by Iris Deng and Ann Cao

data.title: Can Hong Kong’s listing reform 2.0 win over innovative firms?
data.description:
Fang Liu, a partner at global law firm Clifford Chance, counts among his clients many technology companies that are keen to list in Hong Kong, but find the current requirements cumbersome.
Clifford Chance has helped more than a dozen innovative companies raise funds under the new listing regime since 2018, when Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) introduced reforms for pre-revenue biotech firms and companies with weighted voting rights (WVR), where one class of shareholders carries more voting rights than others.
“The current market cap requirement for WVR [firms] is out of reach for the substantial majority of potential listing candidates,” said Fang, whose firm helped 14 companies market their initial public offerings (IPOs) in the first quarter and raise US$5.7 billion.
“The HKEX proposal to lower the market cap requirement will enhance the attractiveness of Hong Kong, as many of the relatively smaller innovative companies now have the option to list here [under the WVR framework],” he said.


Expectations rose anew when the HKEX last month announced its biggest listing reform since 2018. The consultation period for the proposal ends on May 8.
Under the proposed reform, the bourse operator has halved the minimum valuation for WVR companies to HK$20 billion (US$2.6 billion), easing the pathway for overseas-listed issuers to list in Hong Kong, among other enhancements to listing requirements. All listed companies can file confidentially, and they only have to disclose information to the public after receiving listing approval.
“The exchange has received requests to make further efforts to attract the homecoming listings of these Greater China issuers listed overseas, including those with WVR structures, as well as secondary listings of Southeast Asian issuers,” the HKEX consultation paper said.
To attract overseas-listed innovative companies to launch secondary listings, the bourse also proposed lowering the market capitalisation requirement to HK$6 billion from HK$10 billion.
Under the proposal, the HKEX will increase the voting rights cap for holders of premium-­class shares to 20 times those of other shareholders for firms with a market capitalisation of HK$40 billion, up from the existing cap of 10 times. The cap for WVR shareholders will change to about 5 per cent from 10 per cent.
HKEX is also expanding the scope of businesses eligible for listing under the WVR regime by redefining the “innovative” test to allow more non-tech issuers with new business models to list. Currently, only technology and innovative companies qualify.

Veteran investment banker John Lee Chen-kwok and his team at UBS have received several inquiries from potential issuers over the past few weeks.
“Listing candidates want to know more about the proposed change to the listing requirements for WVRs and confidential filing,” said Lee, vice-chairman and co-head of Asia coverage at the Swiss bank, which is one of the largest listing sponsors in the city.
More artificial intelligence, robotics and healthcare companies would seek listings in Hong Kong if the reform went ahead, said Kelvin Leung, Greater China deputy divisional president of CPA Australia, an accountancy body.
“Greater clarity around the proposed framework should make it easier for more of these companies to evaluate Hong Kong seriously as a listing venue,” Leung said.
Since the exchange’s last reform in 2018, the market has continued to evolve. The HKEX set a high bar as it wanted only to attract large WVR companies to prevent being inundated by applicants.
Katherine Ng, head of listing at HKEX, said at the time of announcing the latest consultation that the 2018 listing regime did take into account the issue of corporate governance. It was time to open the doors to more diversified and relatively smaller companies to list, she added.
“If we are not doing anything, we will lose out to other markets,” Ng said.
The reforms are needed to enhance the city’s competitiveness as a top global listing venue, as it faces competition from exchanges in the US, UK, Singapore and Australia, all of which have undertaken listing reforms in recent years.


“Virtually all major markets are updating their listing regimes to stay competitive, so Hong Kong cannot stand still,” said Stephen Law, president of the Hong Kong Institute of Certified Public Accountants (HKICPA) and a private equity investor. “Singapore’s cooperation with Nasdaq, and reforms in the US, UK and Australia underline how intense the global competition for quality listings has become. In that context, the latest Hong Kong proposals are a step in the right direction.”
Global stock exchanges are fighting to attract innovative companies with WVR structures, because many founders – having diluted their stakes to raise early-stage funding – no longer hold a majority of shares.
The 2018 reform turned out to be a game changer, as Hong Kong became a sought-after listing venue for innovative companies. Since the reform came into effect on April 30 of that year, 122 firms raised a total of HK$629 billion, representing 34.3 per cent of the total IPO funds raised up to the end of last year, according to HKEX data.
New-economy companies now represent 48.2 per cent of market capitalisation, up from 14.6 per cent in 2017. In contrast, old-economy firms from the financial, property and construction sectors now account for 27.4 per cent of market capitalisation, compared with 43.3 per cent in 2017.
The HKEX’s success has forced rival exchanges to implement reforms. Shanghai, Shenzhen, Singapore and London now allow companies with WVR to list.
These exchanges have adopted a lower threshold than Hong Kong’s HK$40 billion market cap for WVR firms. UK has set the bar at £30 million (US$39.7 million), Singapore at S$150 million (US$116.7 million), Shanghai’s Star Market and Shenzhen’s ChiNext at 10 billion yuan (US$1.46 billion), New York Stock Exchange at US$750 million and Nasdaq at US$850 million.
Hong Kong’s proposed move to cut the market cap for WVR firms to HK$20 billion could be seen as a defensive approach, according to analysts. Still, HKEX’s requirements would be much higher than those of the US, UK and Singapore, but slightly lower than those of the main boards of Shenzhen and Shanghai, which stand at 20 billion yuan (US$2.9 billion), they added.
The reform comes as HKEX’s main board became the world’s largest IPO market, a title it regained in 2025 and held again in the first quarter of this year, for the first time since 2019.
Hi-tech firms accounted for 49.1 per cent of total fundraising in Hong Kong in the year’s first three months, raising US$6.5 billion from 15 IPOs and secondary listings. This shows that attracting such companies is key to ensuring the city’s top billing.
While the exchange’s data points to a healthy IPO pipeline of about 500 companies, the city could cede its crown to the US, which is expected to see mega listings from companies such as OpenAI and SpaceX this year.
Elon Musk’s SpaceX was likely to launch the world’s biggest IPO, which was expected to raise US$75 billion around the middle of the year and value the company at as much as US$1.75 trillion, according to a Reuters report on Tuesday.

Last November, Singapore Exchange (SGX) tied up with Nasdaq to lift its flagging stock market, establishing a dual-listing bridge between both exchanges to attract IPO candidates as well as companies already listed on the US bourse.
David Chang, founder and CEO of venture capital firm MindWorks, said the HKEX reform was important for many mid-cap technology companies to raise funds.
Chang, however, is not unduly worried about Hong Kong facing pressure from overseas exchanges.
The SGX’s dual-listing bridge, expected to launch later this year and allow listing candidates to file one application for both Singapore and the US, was aimed at attracting Southeast Asian growth companies seeking US capital market access, Chang said.
“These firms do not have the same investor base as Chinese tech issuers and, therefore, are not in direct competition with Hong Kong,” he added.
Reform in the UK was also heading in the right direction, but it did not have the same strong foundation as Hong Kong, Chang said, noting that “London lacks the pool of China-specialist institutional capital that drives Hong Kong valuations, and no regulatory change can manufacture that”.
“The US is not competing for Chinese listings as it is actively deterring them and [many] US-listed Chinese companies have already established a secondary or primary listing in Hong Kong,” he added.
To capture US-listed Chinese and Southeast Asian companies, the HKEX has proposed to lower the minimum market capitalisation to HK$6 billion from HK$10 billion for those seeking a secondary listing in Hong Kong.
“This would be a very attractive proposition for overseas-listed companies, giving them an opportunity to enter the market and benefit from Hong Kong’s deep liquidity,” Clifford Chance’s Fang said.
Of the more than 400 Chinese companies listed in the US at the end of last year, 377 were not listed in Hong Kong, according to HKEX data. Meanwhile, 22 companies from Southeast Asia had listed in the US since 2018, of which 14 were new economy firms, the data showed.
The HKEX has identified 62 US-listed companies with WVRs, including 57 from Greater China and five from Southeast Asia, representing a combined market capitalisation of US$193 billion.
Of these, 15 qualify for listing in Hong Kong under the current rules, while 11 would meet the listing threshold after the proposed rule change. The rest would still not meet the requirements.

While prospective issuers and bankers are positive about the reform, the timing could be off slightly. The strong stock market gains that supported a flurry of IPOs faded in March after the US-Israeli attacks on Iran began on February 28, with the benchmark Hang Seng Index slumping 7 per cent.
Even though the Middle East conflict has affected market sentiment, UBS’ Lee said Hong Kong was ideally suited for innovative firms to list, but some issuers may need more flexibility with regard to the listing requirements.
“The reform is very much awaited by innovative companies which are considering listing in Hong Kong, as the proposed amendments would offer more flexibility and the confidential filing would address potential concerns on competitive positioning,” Lee said.
The reform also comes as Chinese regulators require certain listing candidates not to list via the red-chip structure, which may slow down approvals for mainland firms. Red chips are entities registered overseas but with assets and businesses within China.
CPA Australia’s Leung said that while there was no announcement that all Chinese companies had to abandon the red-chip structure to list, the trend of companies using H shares to list was rising.
Based on HKEX’s data, Leung said 91 per cent of applicants as of March 25 were using the H shares format, compared with 64 per cent in 2025 and 44 per cent in 2024.
“That suggests the listing mix has already been shifting over time towards H-share style structures,” he said. “The H-share route may increasingly be viewed as more straightforward from a regulatory and filing perspective for certain businesses. It does not change Hong Kong’s long-term attractiveness as a leading offshore fundraising venue for Chinese enterprises.”
MindWorks’ Chang also said that the China Securities Regulatory Commission was not banning the red-chip structure but only targeting certain companies.
“H-share listings will skew towards larger, cleaner issuers, which may improve Hong Kong’s average listing quality over time but compress near-term pipeline volume,” Chang said.
“For companies targeting a Hong Kong listing, structure decisions need to be made at series A, not pre-IPO. This is now a front-loaded consideration for any investor in companies likely to take this path.”

data.title: 73 public schools join new scheme to hire native English-speaking teachers
data.description:
More than 70 public schools, or nearly 9 per cent, have joined a new scheme in Hong Kong to hire native English-speaking teachers (NETs) that offers lower pay but higher recruitment flexibility.
Under the scheme launched this academic year, operators were given a grant to hire such teachers, to enhance the effectiveness of English-language instruction and increase students’ exposure to the language.
“In the 2025-26 school year, a total of 73 aided schools – 28 primary schools and 45 secondary schools – opted to receive the NET grant,” a spokeswoman for the Education Bureau told the South China Morning Post.
The 73 account for 8.7 per cent of Hong Kong’s 838 public primary and secondary schools.
Each primary or secondary school that opts for the NET grant receives HK$900,000 (US$114,900) and HK$1 million respectively every academic year under the scheme, covering all costs including basic salaries, contract gratuity and fringe benefits. Schools determine teachers’ pay packages.
Schools can use the grant to hire at least one native English-speaking teacher and qualified teaching assistants, and to subscribe to English learning services run by relevant service providers.
According to the plans of 21 participating secondary and seven primary schools seen by the SCMP, the average monthly pay for secondary school teachers under the scheme was about HK$46,600, while that for primary school teachers was HK$37,500.
These salaries were generally lower than the pay offered under the bureau’s existing grant programme for NETs, which a number of other schools continue to operate under.
At least six secondary and four primary schools hired more than one teacher under the scheme, and 12 out of the 28 schools analysed by the SCMP chose to employ teaching assistants.
One secondary school used the grant to hire three native English-speaking teachers, budgeting HK$930,000 for their salaries, meaning each one earned a monthly wage of about HK$26,000. Similarly, a primary school hired three teachers with HK$850,000, paying each one about HK$24,000 per month.
Some schools were more generous. One secondary school in Tin Shui Wai used HK$999,900 to hire just one teacher, paying her HK$83,325 a month. It did not indicate whether the salary included a housing allowance.
A primary school in Islands district hired one teacher, paying her a monthly wage of HK$60,800 – the highest among all primary schools in the list seen by the SCMP.
“The Education Bureau has received positive feedback from schools on the flexibility of the new scheme to attracting more native English speakers with diverse academic and cultural backgrounds,” the spokeswoman said.
“The scheme enabled schools to form a dynamic team of native-English speaking teachers and teaching assistants to enrich students’ language learning experiences and strengthen the English learning environment.”
Following the introduction of the new scheme, fewer native English-speaking teachers were hired under the old programme in the current academic year than in the last one.

Bureau figures show that about 440 and 340 such teachers hired under the old scheme work in primary and secondary schools, respectively, in the current academic year, compared with 470 and 380 in the last one – an overall reduction of 70 or 8 per cent.
The original NET scheme was implemented in 1998-99 in secondary schools and in 2002-03 in primary schools.
It offered a basic salary according to a points system, and included a contract gratuity, retention incentives and, for teachers who are not from Hong Kong, a special allowance that could be used for housing, baggage and medical fees.
Salaries could range from HK$35,080 to HK$67,850 for primary school teachers, and up to HK$81,510 for those in secondaries, with the special allowance at HK$20,989 per month.
The contract gratuity for the first and second two-year employment period was 10 per cent of the total salary, increasing to 15 per cent after the four years.
Lee Yi-ying, chairwoman of the Hong Kong Subsidised Secondary Schools Council, said the new scheme’s greater flexibility was valued by schools that hired native English-speaking teachers.
“I think if schools do not renew the contracts of current teachers and want to hire new and younger native English speakers in the next school year, they will probably switch to the new grant as it offers them greater flexibility,” Lee, a principal, said.
If the market did not respond well or schools found it harder to hire English-speaking teachers, they could switch back to the older NET scheme and return to a higher remunerative package to hire and retain talent, she added.
